coredata.dc:title: A comparative study on the edge states in phosphorene quantum dots and rings
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Z.T.
Liang, F.X.
Zhang, X.D.
coredata.dc:description: Using the tight-binding Hamiltonian approach, we comparatively investigate the energy spectrums of triangular zigzag phosphorene quantum dots (PQDs) and rings (PQRs), as well as their potential applications. In comparison with the outer edge states in the PQD, new extra inner edge states can be produced in the PQR by its internal hole. A transition from the uncoupled to coupled edge states can be induced by decreasing the width between the outer and inner edges of the PQR. Also, the edge states in PQD/PQR are all anisotropically localized in one side, rather than three sides as in triangular graphene quantum dots (QDs) and rings (QRs). Furthermore, the PQD/PQR energy levels can be anisotropically manipulated by the external electric fields and strains, clearly demonstrating their potential applications in field effect transistors or electromechanical devices. In the meanwhile, we also consider the electron probability distributions corresponding to the different energy levels, clearly exposing the characteristics of the PQD/PQR energy levels. The comparison between the asymmetrical triangular PQDs/PQRs and the symmetrical triangular QDs/QRs in graphene should be instructive for understanding the similar triangular QDs/QRs in other two-dimensional layered materials, as well as other types of QDs/QRs of different shapes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Edge states
Phosphorene
Quantum dots
Quantum rings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(Color online.) Top views of the triangular zigzag PQD (a) and PQR (b). The solid and open circles represent phosphorus atoms in the up and down sublayers, respectively. x (y) axis is along the zigzag (armchair) direction. Schematically shown are the five coupling terms t 0i (i =1,2,…,5), the basis vector lengths a and b of the primitive cell, and the other related in-plane geometry parameters a 0, b 0x , b 0y , and d 0. Also the size of the PQD or PQR is denoted by N
e and the hole size in PQR is by N
i .

(Color online.) (a) Energy levels vs eigenvalue index for the triangular zigzag PQDs of different sizes N
e . (b) Energy levels vs eigenvalue index for the triangular zigzag PQRs of size N
e
=16 in the presence of the holes with different size N
i . Shown in the upper-left/lower-right insets are the zoomed in views of the ZB energy levels/the energy levels of the corresponding PQD or PQR.

(Color online.) The electron probability distribution for the ZB, and the top FB energy level and the bottom UB one of the PQD labeled by 1 and 2 in the lower-right inset of Fig. 2 for the PQD (a, b, c) and the PQR (d, e, f). The small black dots denote the lattice positions, while the areas of the red filled circles the probabilities of finding the electrons on them. (g) [(h)] The curves from left to right represent the ZB energy levels when the hole of N
i
=7 [5] moves from vertex A to side BC in the PQR with N
e
=16 [44], with the far left curve corresponding to the PQD. For comparison, we denote the varied/intact energy levels in relative to the far left curve by the solid/open circles, and the green triangles denotes the energy levels localized near vertex A.

(Color online.) Variations of the energy levels for the PQD of N
e
=16 (a) [(b)] and PQR of N
e
=16 and N
i
=7 (c) [(d)] as a function of the electric field E
x [E
y ]. (e) Variation of the PQD energy levels versus z-direction field E
z locally applied in the proximity of side BC in the PQD. (f), (g), and (h) denote the variations of the PQR energy levels when E
z is locally applied on the outer side BC, the inner side BC, and both in the PQR, respectively.

(Color online.) Energy levels versus the strain indices ε
x,y,z along the x, y, and z directions for the PQD of N
e
=16 (a, b, c) and the PQR of N
e
=16 and N
i
=7 (d, e, f).

(Color online.) Total ZB electron probability distributions of the PQD (N
e
=16)/PQR (N
e
=16 and N
i
=7) under the compressed strain (a, c)/(e, g) and the tensile one (b, d)/(f, h). The small black dots denote the lattice positions, while the areas of the red filled circles the probabilities of finding the electrons on them.

Coefficients α
ix,y,z for different interatom couplings.

head.ce:title: A comparative study on the edge states in phosphorene quantum dots and rings

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using the tight-binding Hamiltonian approach, we comparatively investigate the energy spectrums of triangular zigzag phosphorene quantum dots (PQDs) and rings (PQRs), as well as their potential applications. In comparison with the outer edge states in the PQD, new extra inner edge states can be produced in the PQR by its internal hole. A transition from the uncoupled to coupled edge states can be induced by decreasing the width between the outer and inner edges of the PQR. Also, the edge states in PQD/PQR are all anisotropically localized in one side, rather than three sides as in triangular graphene quantum dots (QDs) and rings (QRs). Furthermore, the PQD/PQR energy levels can be anisotropically manipulated by the external electric fields and strains, clearly demonstrating their potential applications in field effect transistors or electromechanical devices. In the meanwhile, we also consider the electron probability distributions corresponding to the different energy levels, clearly exposing the characteristics of the PQD/PQR energy levels. The comparison between the asymmetrical triangular PQDs/PQRs and the symmetrical triangular QDs/QRs in graphene should be instructive for understanding the similar triangular QDs/QRs in other two-dimensional layered materials, as well as other types of QDs/QRs of different shapes.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional materials ranging from NbSe2 [1], graphene [2], MoS2 [3], hexagonal boron nitride [4], and transitional-metal dichalcogenides [5] to phosphorene [6] have triggered overwhelming interest in the low-dimensional research field in recent years. Especially, phosphorene, a single layer of black phosphorus, has started to gain more and more attention due to its finite bandgap [6–8], high carrier mobility [9,10], and large on-off current ratios [10–12]. In spite of its difficulty in material fabrication, phosphorene is still looked upon as a promising candidate in the race for the electronic, thermoelectric, and plasmonic applications. As a newcomer of the two-dimensional material family that can be experimentally realized also by exfoliation [6,12], the initial research focus about phosphorene has been concentrated on the determination of its band structure [13–15], as well as the practicable means of band manipulation [15–21]. It has been demonstrated that the bandgap can be adjusted by changing the layer number [8,14,15], applying the external electric field [16], or imposing the strains [17]. In comparison with the planar graphene, phosphorene of the puckered lattice structure is more flexible and durable, and even able to sustain up to about 30% deformation both in zigzag and armchair directions [18].

Up to now, most of the researches were carried out in the ideal single or few layer phosphorene systems of infinite size. However, it is inevitable to constrain phosphorene to form one-dimensional phosphorene nanoribbon or zero-dimensional phosphorene quantum dots (PQDs) so as to design phosphorene-based functional devices for various applications. Very recently, the investigation on the properties of the phosphorene nanoribbons have begun [22–28]. However, to the best of our knowledge, only two research works on the PQDs have been done. One is the experimental synthesis of the few-layer PQDs based on chemical methods by Zhang et al. [29], whereby a flexible memory device was successfully fabricated that exhibits a nonvolatile rewritable memory effect with a high on-off current ratio and good stability. The other is the theoretical study on the five types of the monolayer PQDs, including triangular, rectangular, and hexangular PQDs with armchair or zigzag edges by Zhang et al. [30]. They studied the energy levels and density of states in the different PQDs of different sizes, as well as the influences of the magnetic field. However, the influences of the electric field, the strains, and so on, have not been discussed. On the other hand, it has been demonstrated that holes in graphene quantum rings (GQRs) will produce new edge states in the GQRs [31–33], different from those in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [34–36]. One may naturally wonder whether there are similar new edge states appearing in PQRs in comparison with the corresponding PQDs. In addition, the electron probability distributions are symmetrically localized on three sides for the triangular GQDs or GQRs. Geometrically, the triangular GQDs or GQRs can be readily designed with the three sides having the same edges. However, there is no way to make the triangular PQDs/PQRs have three completely same sides due to the phosphorene anisotropy. Therefore, it should be instructive to investigate, between the asymmetrical PQDs/PQRs and symmetrical GQDs/GQRs, what kind of differences exist in the aspects such as the energy levels, the edge states, the effect of the hole in PQRs, as well as their potential application in device designing.

Motivated by these researches, in this paper we design one kind of triangular zigzag PQDs and PQRs, as shown in Fig. 1 , to carry out a comparative study on their energy spectrums. It is found that the energy levels of the triangular zigzag PQDs/PQRs are assembled in the full-filled band (FB), the zero-energy band (ZB), and the unoccupied band (UB), and further ZB are found to be edge states while FB and UB are not according to the electron probability distributions. It is demonstrated that the holes can produce new edge states in the PQRs, making serious influences on the energy spectrum in relative to those of the PQDs. Both the electric field and mechanical strains can make the anisotropic influences on their spectrums of the PQDs/PQRs, demonstrating the potential applications in designing the electronic or electromechanical devices. Moreover, the specific distributions of the electron probabilities are selectively presented to deepen the understanding about the FB, ZB, and UB. In the meanwhile, the necessary comparisons between PQDs/PQRs and GQDs/GQRs are made, and the applicability of the PQD/PQR-based devices and the universality of the conclusion drawn from the triangular PQDs/PQRs are discussed.

This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. 2, we present the tight-binding Hamiltonian of the PQD and PQR. A complete numerical investigation into the PQD/PQR energy levels, as well as the influences of the electric field and the strain, is carried out in Sec. 3. Finally, a brief conclusion is given in Sec. 4.

The structures of the triangular zigzag PQD and PQR considered in this paper are schematically shown in Figs. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively. The PQD is composed of one normal zigzag side BC and two skewed zigzag sides AB and AC, and the PQR can be viewed as a PQD with a central hole. Obviously, along BC direction the up and down sublayer atoms separately constitute one-dimensional horizontal zigzag atom chains represented by the solid blue circles and the open red ones, respectively. For each zigzag atom chain, they can be viewed as a compound lattice including two types of atoms localized on the down-sawtooth (A-type atoms) and up-sawtooth (B-type atoms) terminal positions. The size of the triangular PQD is described by its side length
N
e
(the number of the atoms in side BC), which indicates that the total number of the phosphorus atoms in the PQD is
N
e
2
+ 4
N
e
+ 1 . Since there are
N
i
2
− 2
N
i
+ 1 atoms in the hole of size length
N
i
for the triangular PQR in Fig. 1(b), the total number of the phosphorus atoms left in the PQR is equal to
N
e
2
−
N
i
2
+ 2 ( 2
N
e
+
N
i
) . The tight-binding Hamiltonian of the PQD or PQR can be written as [14,15]
(1)
H =
∑ i
ϵ
i
c
i
†
c
i
+
∑
i ≠ j
t
i j
c
i
†
c
j
,
where
c
i
†
(
c
i
) is the creation (annihilation) operator of the electron at atom i,
ϵ
i
the on-site energy of atom i, and
t
i j
the hopping coupling between atoms i and j. According to Ref. [14], it is a good approximation to take into account the five hopping couplings
t
01
,
t
02
,
t
03
,
t
04
, and
t
05
, which are −1.22 eV, 3.665 eV, −0.205 eV, −0.105 eV, and −0.055 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(a). Correspondingly, the interatom distances are denoted by
r
01
,
r

,
r

,
r

, and
r
05
, which can be expressed by using the parameters
a
0
,
b
0 x , y
,
d
0 x , y
, and
l
0
. Note that
d
0 x , y
are the x , y -direction components of
d
0
, and
l
0
the vertical distance between the two sublayers. When the PQD/PQR structure is deformed by a strain, the hopping coupling
t
i
will be modulated as [37]
(2)
t
i
/
t
0 i
≈ 1 − 2 (
α
i x
ε
i x
+
α
i y
ε
i y
+
α
i z
ε
i z
) .
Here
ε
i x , y , z
denote the strain indices applied along the x , y , z -directions with the coefficients
α
i x
( =
x
0 i
2
r
0 i
− 2
) ,
α
i y
( =
y

2
r

− 2
) , and
α
i z
( =
z
0 i
2
r
0 i
− 2
) listed in Table 1 . Moreover, the related geometry parameters in Fig. 1 are
a
0
= 0.7054
Å ,
b
0 x
= 1.6570
Å ,
b
0 y
= 1.4826
Å ,
d
0 x
= 1.6570
Å ,
d
0 y
= 2.1880
Å , and
l
0
= 2.1312
Å .

In this section, we will carry out a numerical study on the energy levels of the triangular zigzag PQDs and PQRs. We consider the influences on the energy levels induced by the geometry tailoring, the electrical field, and the strains. In order to recognize the characteristics of the energy levels, we also selectively show the electron probability distributions of the PQD/PQR energy levels. Moreover, we emphasize the possibility of some potential designing schemes of the PQD/PQR-based devices.

First of all, we show the energy levels of the PQD/PQR in Fig. 2 . It can be found that the energy levels are assembled in three regions, forming three bands FB, ZB, and UB, with bandgap 1 (2) between FB (UB) and ZB. Obviously, the energy levels of FB and UB are not symmetrical about ZB, and bandgap 1 is greater than bandgap 2 for a certain PQD or PQR. As clearly shown in the upper-left inset, the energy levels in ZB are all nondegenerate, different from the rigidly degenerate case in symmetrical triangular GQDs and GQRs [34]. With the increase of the PQD side length
N
e
, all the FB, ZB, and UB bandwidths become larger and the two bandgaps will become smaller, as shown in Fig. 2(a). A rigorous counting shows that there are (
N
e
2
+ 3
N
e
) / 2 ,
N
e
+ 1 , and (
N
e
2
+ 3
N
e
) / 2 energy levels in FB, ZB, and UB, respectively. This indicates that the same number of energy levels appears in FB and UB, and the energy level number in ZB, except one at vertex A as discussed below, is equal to the number
N
e
of the outmost edge atoms in the BC side. Then we discuss the PQR energy levels in Fig. 2(b) for comparison. Clearly, with increasing the hole length
N
i
, the ZB bandwidth becomes wider while the FB and UB bandwidths become narrower, and finally both bandgaps 1 and 2 will be narrowed. Consequently, there are (
N
e
2
+ 3
N
e
−
N
i
2
+
N
i
) / 2 ,
N
e
+
N
i
, and (
N
e
2
+ 3
N
e
−
N
i
2
+
N
i
) / 2 energy levels found in FB, ZB, and UB, indicating that the presence of the hole of size
N
i
will increase the ZB energy level number by
N
i
− 1 and suppress the FB and UB energy level numbers by (
N
i
2
−
N
i
) / 2 , respectively. In this case, the total energy level number in ZB except one at vertex A is equal to the total number of the atoms in the outmost and inmost edge atoms in the BC side. This clearly demonstrates that the hole can produce extra
N
i
− 1 energy levels in the ZB of the PQR in comparison with the corresponding PQD. By comparing Fig. 2(a) with 2(b), we further can draw a conclusion that the increases of
N
e
and
N
i
will increase the ZB bandwidth and decrease the two bandgaps in the similar way, while increase or decrease the FB and UB bandwidths in an opposite way.

In order to clearly elucidate the energy level properties, we show the electron probability distributions of the ZB, the top FB energy level (labeled 1 in the lower-right inset of Fig. 2), and the bottom UB one (labeled 2 in the lower-right inset of Fig. 2) for the PQDs in Figs. 3 (a–c) and the PQRs in 3(d–f), respectively. We can see in Fig. 3(a) that the total ZB probability distribution is mainly distributed in the regions of vertex A and edge BC, sharply different from the equilateral distribution patterns in symmetrical triangular GQDs. [34,36] This indicates that the energy levels in the ZB correspond to the edge-states, in agreement with the results in Ref. [30]. However, the state of
E
1
in FB and that of
E
2
in UB are mainly distributed in the inner body regions, see Figs. 3(b) and 3(c). Also, we have calculated the total probability distributions of the FB and UB, which are found to be localized mainly in the regions out of vertex A and edge BC (not shown here). In addition, we can find that the electron probability distributions for ZB only appear on the A-type atom positions near vertex A, and on the B-type atom positions near edge BC. However, those of the FB and UB can simultaneously appear in A- and B-type atom positions. This clearly demonstrates that the probability distributions of the ZB are convened on A- or B-type atom positions in the manner different from those of the FB and UB.

Different from that in Fig. 3(a), the ZB probability distribution of the PQR shown in Fig. 3(d) appears not only in vertex A and the outer edge parallel to the side BC, but also the inner edge along BC. In fact, except one special energy level with its electron probability localized near vertex A, all the other energy levels in ZB have their electron probabilities distributed in the region ranging from the inner to outer edge along side BC. Furthermore, we move the hole from vertex A to side BC to consider the influences induced by the hole position, and it is found that more energy levels are inclined to be changed due to the couplings between two edges [see Fig. 3(g)]. For the flexibility in changing the hole position, an enough large PQR with a small hole is investigated in Fig. 3(h) by plotting their ZB energy levels. Obviously, when the hole is close to (or far away from) vertex A (or side BC),
N
i
− 1 edges states belonging to the inner BC edge appears with all the original energy levels belonging to the outer BC edge invariant except for that near vertex A. This clearly demonstrates that the newly added energy levels in ZB are mainly attributed to the inner edge along BC. In the small width limit between the inner and outer BC edges, almost all the energy levels in BC edges are changed due to the couplings between two BC edges. This implies that a transition from the uncoupled to coupled energy levels will occur with decreasing the width between the inner and outer edges. Certainly for an intermediate width, one can expect there coexist the coupled and uncoupled energy levels. Therefore, the energy levels in ZB will be classified into three kinds: the inner-edge energy levels, the outer-edge energy levels, and the inner-outer-edge coupled energy levels. Moreover, we can find that for the inner-edge (outer-edge) energy levels, their electron probability distribution appears only on the A-type (B-type) atom positions near the inner (outer) edge. However, for the coupled energy levels, their probability distributions appear both in A-type atom region near the inner edge and B-type atom region near the outer one, and their overlap between two regions vividly reflects the coupling between the inner and outer edges. This probability distribution should be an important reference for understanding the properties of the PQD/PQR energy levels. Furthermore, one can expect that a different orientation of the hole will make different influences on the ZB, indicating the manipulation of the hole on the PQR energy levels is anisotropic.

Now we investigate how the PQD/PQR energy levels are controlled by external electrical fields. First of all, as usual a back gate can be used to lift up or lower the whole energy levels of the PQD/PQR, indicating the PQD/PQR sandwiched between two leads with a bias voltage can be tuned in the on or off state depending on the bias window in alignment with the FB, ZB, UB, or two bandgaps. This indicates that the PQD/PQR can be used as a field effect transistor (FET). Though the bandgaps of the PQR are smaller than those of the corresponding PQD, the inner edge states can be designed to make no contribution to the transport along side BC, indicating it is still suitable to act as a FET. For convenience, here we can suppose that the linear bias window is set to be in the proximity of the zero energy. Then we study how x , y -direction electric fields control the bands of the PQD [Figs. 4 (a,b)] and the PQR [Figs. 4(c, d)]. We can expect that the electron transport via ZB will be prohibited in a certain scope of y-direction electric field as shown in Figs. 4(b, d), while it is not for the x-direction one in Figs. 4(a, c). This indicates that the PQD can act as a FET under the y-direction electric field, rather than the x-direction one. Also, it is clear that the off-state scope can be suppressed due to the presence of the hole by comparing Fig. 4(d) with 4(b), and the only energy level almost immune to the electric fields is attributed to the vertex A. Next, in view of the probability distribution of the ZB energy levels we consider the effects of a locally applied z-direction electric field on the energy bands in Figs. 4(e–f). As shown in Fig. 4(e), the local electric field applied near the side BC in PQD can effectively adjust the ZB with a larger off-state bias scope in relative to that in Fig. 4(a), since the field has little effect on the FB and UB. This is also the case for the PQR in Fig. 4(h), where the electrical field is applied between inner and outer edges. Thus it is demonstrated that a local electric field is more effective to turn on or off the transport through the ZB channel. Furthermore, we can apply the electric field locally on the outer or inner BC side, as shown in Figs. 4(f) and 4(g), respectively. We can see that the separate manipulation of the two edge states along side BC can be established, indicating that the electron can selectively transport through side BC via the outer edge, the inner one, or both. Therefore, we can draw a conclusion that the PQD/PQR can be used as a FET under a back gate, a y-direction electric field, or a local z-direction electric field, and the electron can be tuned to selectively pass through the inner or outer edges of side BC in the PQR. However, one has to acknowledge that the local scheme of applying the electric field to the PQD or PQR is technically rigorous.

Fig. 5 shows the variations of the energy levels of the PQD/PQR as a function of the strain index. Note that a positive (negative) strain index means a tensile (compressed) strain. Wholly speaking, the total bandwidth ranging from the FB bottom to the UB top will be decreased with the increase of
ε
x , y , z
. The ZB is kept in the proximity of the zero energy, while the FB and UB are inclined to approach the ZB. Specifically speaking, the FB, ZB, and UB bandwidths become narrower separately and both bandgaps 1 and 2 become wider with the increase of the strain indexes
ε
x , y
. However, with increasing
ε
z
, the FB and UB bandwidths are changed slightly before their amalgamation with ZB and the two bandgaps become smaller or even vanish. This indicates that the strain can make the anisotropic effect on the energy bands of the PQD. Moreover, by comparing Figs. 5(d, e, f) with 5(a, b, c), we can find that the two bandgaps of the PQR with a hole is much easier to be closed than those of the PQD without a hole, which is reasonable since the presence of hole will narrow the two gaps. Also, it is found that the new inner edge states in ZB are more sensitive to the strains than the outer edge states. To clearly explain what on earth bring into being the phenomena shown in Fig. 5, we may resort to the variations of the inter-atom couplings. With the decreasing (increasing) of the compressed (tensile) strain strength, the coupling
t
i
will be suppressed correspondingly. A universal intuitive law is that the bandwidth will become narrower with the decreasing of
t
i
. When
t
1 , 2 , ⋯ , 5
are changed simultaneously, the whole variation of the energy band is contributed by the five kinds of couplings. Thus it is demonstrated that
ε
x , y
can adjust the FB, ZB, UB, and the two bandgaps in different or even opposite ways from
ε
z
. Fundamentally, this should be caused by the positive and negative signs of the couplings, in agreement with the predicted results for the phosphorene [37].

In order to understand the energy level properties of the PQDs/PQRs under the strains, we plot the electron probability distributions of the ZB in Fig. 6 . In comparison with the distribution of the pristine PQD shown in Fig. 3(a), the compressed strain [with
ε
x
= − 0.2 in Fig. 6(a)] will expand the probability distribution region towards the PQD center and the tensile one [with
ε
x
= 0.15 in Fig. 6(b)] will shrink the probability distribution region towards vertex A or edge BC. However, the probability distributions are kept invariantly on the A-type or B-type atom positions, independent of the applied x-direction strain. Furthermore, we consider the influence of the z-direction strain on the entire probability distributions of the ZB in Figs. 6(c, d). Conspicuously, the probability distributions show the opposite dependence on the applied strain
ε
z
. That is to say, the compressed (tensile) strain will shrink (expand) the probability distribution region. The fundamental reason of leading to the appearance of this opposition may be attributed to the positive or negative signs of the couplings, as pointed out before. In Figs. 6(e–f), we show the electron probability distributions of the PQR with a hole under the different strains. Obviously, the compressed strain along the x-direction will expand the probability distribution near the inner (outer) edge towards the outer (inner) one, resulting in the strongly coupled energy levels, see Fig. 6(e). However, the tensile strain along the x-direction will be inclined to push the probability distributions near the inner and outer edges away from each other, leading to the weakly coupled energy levels, see Fig. 6(f). Similar to that of the PQD shown in Figs. 6(c, d), the variation of the probability distribution versus
ε
z
is opposite to that on
ε
x
, as shown in Figs. 6(g, h). It should be pointed out that the y-direction strain plays the same role in controlling the PQD energy levels as the x-direction strain, whereas the degree of the expansion or the shrink of the probability distribution region induced by
ε
y
is different from that by
ε
x
(not shown here). Therefore, we can draw a conclusion that the strains can control the PQD/PQR energy levels anisotropically, further indicating that it is feasible to design some electromechanical devices. However, in practical device fabrication, how to assemble all the device components including the PQD/PQR, the electrodes, the gates, the elastic materials or substrates, and so on is a complex systematical engineering.

Before ending our research, we would like to first make an objective and more detailed evaluation on the applicability of the designing schemes. In principle, as pointed out before, the FET can be viewed as a central channel (e.g. a PQD/PQR) sandwiched between two leads biased by a small voltage. When the energy level of the PQD/PQR channel is tuned in the bias window, the FET will be in on-state, otherwise in off-state whenever the PQD/PQR bandgap is aligned with the bias window. In this PQD/PQR FET, all three bands can be used as the transport channels and the relatively large bandgaps are beneficial to obtaining the large on-off current ratio. The high adjustability of the energy bands shown in Fig. 4 ensures the feasibility of using the PQD/PQR as the FET. Also, in Fig. 5 the compressed x , y -direction strain and the tensile z-direction one are all able to close the bandgaps, which indicates we can switch the on-off state of the FET by strain. In other words, the strains can control the on-off state, indicating PQD/PQR can be used as the electromechanical device. Therefore, from the theoretical aspect, these designing schemes should be completely feasible. However, in practical experimental process, whether the PQD/PQR device can work closely depends on the fabrication of the high-quality PQD/PQR samples. Especially, if one want to design the ZB-based FET devices, the edge of the PQD/PQR must be rigidly perfect and kept unpassivated by suitable encapsulation technology, which should be challenging. Actually, this rigorous restriction on the edge atom arrangement, common for the similar devices based on all other two-dimensional layered materials, [38,39] seems to be unavoidable on the way of designing the ZB-based PQD/PQR devices in order to use its distinctive anisotropy of phosphorene. Then we want to stress the universality of the results obtained from the triangular PQD/PQR for the PQDs/PQRs of other shapes. The triangular PQD/PQR shape, as well as its three sides depending on the direction in which we cut phosphorene, makes a decisive influence on whether and where the edge states appear. The number of the energy levels in ZB is determined by the number of the atoms in a certain side edge. If the triangular PQD/PQR is cut in different way so that its two sides can host two kinds of edge states, its energy levels in ZB can be approximately viewed as an amalgamation of two separate ZBs belonging to two sides with each behaving as the above discussion about the PQD/PQR with only one side having edge states. These conclusions can be readily extended to other types of the PQDs/PQRs such as the rectangular, hexagonal, and polygonal PQDs/PQRs, where the ZB of the total PQDs/PQRs is composed of more separate ZBs belonging to the corresponding sides. If the polygonal PQDs/PQRs are of certain rotation symmetry, the symmetry will be reflected in the electron probability distributions in the PQDs/PQRs. Therefore, this research should be an important reference for understanding the properties of the polygonal PQDs/PQRs.

In conclusion, we have studied comparatively the energy levels of the triangular PQDs and PQRs. It is shown that the energy levels including the FB, ZB, and UB can be anisotropically manipulated by the hole, the electric field, and the strain. The hole can adjust the energy level number in every band and change the bandgaps, and the electric fields can enable the PQD/PQR to work as a FET. Also, the strains can make serious influences on the PQD/PQR energy levels. Moreover, the properties of the PQD/PQR energy levels and the manipulations on them can be deeply understood according to their probability distributions. The designing schemes of the PQD/PQR-based devices are feasible in principle, and an extension of the study from on the triangular PQD/PQR to the PQDs/PQRs of other shapes has been made.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant Nos. 11274040 and 11574031.
